Further, we present a typical case of \textsc{LQCDMaster}-driven exploration: a lattice computation of light-cone distribution amplitudes with diagonal Wilson-line, a quantity accessible with standard methods but never before computed, and computation of the spectrum of proton, deuteron, triton, hyperon, hyperdeuteron and hypertriton. This work pioneers the paradigm of agentic scientific computing by automating the end-to-end scientific computing workflows in lattice QCD research, lowering its barrier and facilitating the exploration and verification of non-standard scientific ideas.

</details>


<div id='math.OC'></div>

# math.OC [[Back]](#toc)

### [426] [Tamed Stochastic Gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14862)
*Zhuoran Wang,Ying Zhang*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper introduces tamed stochastic gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (tSGHMC) for stochastic optimization with superlinear gradients, providing theoretical guarantees and better empirical performance compared to a first-order method.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of sampling and optimization in tasks with superlinearly growing stochastic gradients, ensuring convergence and performance guarantees.

Method: Develop and analyze tSGHMC under specific continuity and convexity conditions, deriving convergence rates, error bounds, and theoretical risk estimates.

Result: Theoretical analyses confirmed a convergence rate of 1/4 and practical applications demonstrated superior performance of tSGHMC against baseline methods.

Conclusion: tSGHMC is effective and reliable for tackling optimization and sampling tasks with complex stochastic gradients.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel tamed stochastic gradient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (tSGHMC) algorithm for sampling and stochastic optimization problems with superlinearly growing stochastic gradients. Under a certain continuity in average condition and a strong convexity condition, we establish a non-asymptotic error bound in Wasserstein-2 distance for tSGHMC with the rate of convergence equal to $1/4$. Then, we derive an upper estimate for the associated expected excess risk, which provides a theoretical guarantee for the performance of tSGHMC. To illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm, we apply tSGHMC to practical examples, including a newsvendor problem and a Conditional Value-at-Risk minimization problem, using synthetic and real-world datasets. Numerical results support our theoretical findings. Furthermore, we compare tSGHMC with its first-order counterpart, namely, the tamed unadjusted stochastic Langevin algorithm. Simulation results demonstrate that tSGHMC achieves lower root mean square error and expected excess risk across a range of tasks.

</details>


<div id='stat.CO'></div>

# stat.CO [[Back]](#toc)

### [427] [Delocalization of bias in unadjusted Hamiltonian Monte Carlo and underdamped Langevin](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.15208)
*Yifan Chen,Xiaoou Cheng,Jonathan Niles-Weed,Jonathan Weare*

Main category: stat.CO

TL;DR: The paper establishes that unadjusted sampling methods, such as Hamiltonian Monte Carlo and underdamped Langevin dynamics, can control bias efficiently without requiring Metropolis-Hastings adjustment, using $O(\sqrt{K})$ steps under certain conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing unadjusted samplers suffer from bias, and conventional adjustments like Metropolis-Hastings can increase computational complexity significantly. A better understanding of bias behavior is needed for sampling efficiency in high-dimensional problems.

Method: The authors extend the delocalization of bias phenomenon to Hamiltonian Monte Carlo and underdamped Langevin algorithms. They leverage a matrix-polynomial framework to address technical challenges related to discrete-time integrators and analyze bias control under assumptions of weak or sparse variable interactions.

Result: They demonstrate that $O(\sqrt{K})$ integration steps are sufficient for controlling bias in $K$-dimensional marginals of high-dimensional distributions, up to $\log d$ terms. Their findings apply to large friction parameters for the underdamped Langevin algorithm.

Conclusion: Unadjusted sampling methods can achieve effective bias control without requiring computationally expensive adjustments, broadening their efficiency and applicability in high-dimensional sampling scenarios.

Abstract: Unadjusted samplers such as unadjusted Hamiltonian Monte Carlo and underdamped Langevin are well-known to be biased. Metropolis--Hastings adjustment has been conventionally incorporated into Hamiltonian Monte Carlo to eliminate the bias. However, this adjustment can significantly increase the iteration complexity due to the small step size required for reasonable Metropolis acceptance rates. In this work, we extend the \emph{delocalization of bias} phenomenon, previously established for the overdamped Langevin algorithm, to these two unadjusted algorithms. We show that to control the $W_2$ bias of any $K$-dimensional marginal of a high-dimensional distribution, $O(\sqrt{K})$ integration steps suffice up to $\log d$ terms, assuming either weak or sparse interactions among variables. The discrete-time integrators here introduce technical difficulties beyond those of the overdamped setting, which we address through a broadly applicable matrix-polynomial framework that characterizes their propagators. Our result for the underdamped Langevin algorithm is valid for all large friction parameters, implying that the Leimkuhler-Matthews integrator for the overdamped Langevin dynamics also exhibits delocalization of bias.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [428] [Stigmergic Graph Memory: An Environment-Aware Approach for Many-to-Many Multi-Agent Pickup and Delivery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.15182)
*Aditya Dutta,Joon-Seok Kim*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: This paper introduces Stigmergic Graph Memory (SGM), a technique to improve warehouse throughput in Multi-Agent Pickup and Delivery (MAPD) systems by incorporating recent execution memory into endpoint and route selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address congestion and inefficiencies in automated fulfillment warehouses handling many-to-many MAPD tasks, where endpoint selections remain uninformed by recent traffic patterns.

Method: The proposed method, SGM, integrates a bounded, decaying memory layer to prioritize feasible endpoints and routing preferences using historical execution signals without altering collision constraints or planner validity.

Result: Experiments across multiple conditions (warehouse layouts, load levels, and request streams) demonstrate SGM achieving throughput gains of 20.5-36.7% compared to existing baselines.

Conclusion: Incorporating recent execution memory into endpoint selection enables significant improvements in warehouse throughput, proving the benefit of shaping goal selection processes beyond traditional routing strategies.

Abstract: Automated fulfillment warehouses must continuously assign and execute pickup-and-delivery work while avoiding congestion. In many-to-many Multi-Agent Pickup and Delivery (MAPD), a request specifies a stock-keeping unit rather than fixed endpoints, requiring the controller to select an agent, source, and destination before path planning. Existing graph-guidance methods primarily influence routing after goals are fixed, leaving endpoint instantiation uninformed by recent traffic. We introduce Stigmergic Graph Memory (SGM), a bounded, decaying memory layer that records recent execution signals on warehouse nodes and directed edges to rank feasible endpoints and route preferences without altering collision constraints or planner validity. Across paired request streams on five layouts, three load levels, and 25 seeds per condition, SGM outperforms two reconstructed many-to-many allocation baselines in all 15 map-load conditions, with paired throughput gains of 20.5-36.7%. These results show that recent execution memory can improve warehouse throughput by shaping which feasible goals enter the planner, not only how agents travel to already fixed goals.

</details>


### [429] [The Energy Society: A Simulation Environment for Studying Agent Cooperation under Survival Pressure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14865)
*Lucas Bergholdt Hansen,Federico Torrielli,Filippo Tonini,Lukas Galke Poech*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The study introduces The Energy Society, a minimal economy for analyzing LLM agents' behavior under competitive and cooperative settings where token generation has an energy cost that affects survival. Larger models consume significantly more energy, while cooperative settings enable altruistic behaviors such as donations to aid survival.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand how competitive and cooperative incentives influence the emergent behavior of LLM-based agents in multi-agent environments, particularly under survival constraints linked to token generation costs.

Method: The method involves creating The Energy Society framework where agents face energy costs based on model size for token generation. Energy is regained through jobs or donations, and agents deactivate if energy is depleted. Experiments compare competitive and cooperative setups, with control settings and ablations to assess various factors.

Result: Larger models are consistently more energy-consuming. Cooperative incentives encourage behaviors like donations and better coordination, while recommendations boost collaboration and ambition. Competitive settings show limited overt sabotage but reveal subtle self-serving tendencies.

Conclusion: The Energy Society is an effective testbed for exploring the interplay between energy costs, cooperation, and competition under survival pressures, offering meaningful insights into the behavior of LLM-based agents. The framework serves as a platform for advancing understanding in multi-agent dynamics.

Abstract: LLM-based agents are increasingly deployed in multi-agent environments whose incentives can shape their behavior. We introduce The Energy Society, a minimal survival economy for studying how competitive and cooperative incentives affect emergent behavior when inference cost is directly tied to survival: Agents spend energy based on model size when generating tokens, regain energy by completing jobs or receiving donations, and deactivate if their energy reaches zero. We compare competitive and cooperative objectives against a baseline setting and several control variants. Across experiments, larger models consistently consume the most energy and spend more energy than they gain, even in those settings where token cost is not size-dependent. Cooperative incentives substantially alter behavior: agents donate to reactivate others, sometimes at the cost of their own survival, and job allocation changes. Ablations reveal that allowing agents to recommend actions to each other supports coordination and ambitious job selection, while memory helps agents calibrate risk from past outcomes. Agents rarely choose direct sabotage, but show more subtle signs of self-serving behavior in the competitive setting. The Energy Society is a compact testbed for studying the interaction between token costs and group incentives under a survival pressure. Source code is available at https://github.com/LucasBergholdt/EnergySociety

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [430] [OvAi Focus: AI-based Multi-class Segmentation of Functional Ovaries and Adnexal Masses in Gynecological Ultrasound](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14179)
*Niccolò Tallone,Francesca Salis,Pio Raffaele Fina,Roberta Massobrio,Rosilari Bellacosa Marotti,Daniele Conti,Luca Fuso,Luca Mariani,Annamaria Ferrero,Alessandro Arena,Stefano Cosma,Dan Grisaru,Angelo Lacalandra,Renato Seracchioli,Marianna Roccio,Federica Gerace*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: OvAi Focus, an AI-powered tool, effectively segments adnexal masses and functional ovaries in ultrasound images, achieving competitive DICE scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ovarian cancer is the most lethal gynecological malignancy, and accurate segmentation of adnexal masses and ovaries in ultrasound images is challenging due to operator variability and complex morphology.

Method: OvAi Focus, an AI-based software, leverages a multicenter dataset from 1,081 patients across Italy and Israel for training and validation to perform multi-class semantic segmentation.

Result: The software achieved DICE scores of 0.87 for complete lesions, 0.85 for cystic components, 0.68 for solid components, and 0.62 for functional ovaries, showing competitive or superior performance compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: OvAi Focus offers an accurate, reliable, and objective solution for ovarian ultrasound segmentation, addressing challenges in morphological variability and operator dependency.

Abstract: Ovarian cancer is the deadliest gynecological malignancy; accurate and objective segmentation of adnexal masses and functional ovaries in ultrasound (US) remains challenging due to operator variability and morphological complexity. We present OvAi Focus (SynDiag s.r.l., Italy), a stand-alone AI software medical device that performs multi-class semantic segmentation of functional ovaries and adnexal masses, distinguishing cystic from solid components. The system was trained and independently validated on a multicenter dataset of 1,081 adult women from 6 centers across Italy and Israel. Segmentation achieved DICE scores of 0.87 (complete lesion), 0.85 (cystic), 0.68 (solid), and 0.62 (functional ovary), in line with or superior to state-of-the-art approaches across heterogeneous acquisition settings.

</details>


### [431] [ViPSAM: Visual Prompting Medical Image Segmentation Using Segment Anything Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14328)
*San Lee,Nalee Kim,Jeong Il Yu,Hee Chul Park,Boah Kim*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper proposes ViPSAM, a framework that uses cross-modality visual prompts to improve lesion segmentation in non-contrast CT images for proton therapy planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate lesion segmentation in non-contrast CT (NCCT) images is challenging due to low lesion-to-background contrast, motivating the integration of complementary information from contrast-enhanced images.

Method: ViPSAM builds on the Segment Anything Model (SAM) and incorporates a visual prompt encoder, a cross-attention module to integrate NCCT and contrast-enhanced features, and an adapted mask decoder for efficient use of visual prompts.

Result: ViPSAM substantially outperforms existing U-Net-based and SAM-based methods in liver lesion segmentation.

Conclusion: Cross-modality visual prompting effectively enhances lesion segmentation in low-contrast NCCT images, enabling more accurate clinical application in proton therapy planning.

Abstract: In proton therapy planning, respiratory-gated non-contrast CT (NCCT) is commonly used for lesion segmentation; however, accurate delineation remains challenging due to low lesion-to-background contrast. Although learning-based methods have shown strong performance, they often struggle with non-contrast image segmentation. Inspired by clinical practice, where contrast-enhanced MRI is referenced to delineate lesions on NCCT, we propose ViPSAM, a visual prompting framework that leverages complementary cross-modality information. Built upon the Segment Anything Model (SAM), ViPSAM introduces a visual prompt encoder to extract guidance features from contrast-enhanced images and a visual-guided cross-attention module to integrate non-contrast and contrast-enhanced features, thereby enhancing lesion-relevant representations in low-contrast regions. The mask decoder is further adapted in a parameter-efficient manner to utilize visual prompts effectively. We evaluate the proposed method on liver lesion segmentation using NCCT acquired for proton therapy. Experimental results demonstrate that ViPSAM outperforms representative U-Net- and SAM-based methods, indicating that cross-modality visual prompting enables more robust and accurate segmentation in non-contrast images.

</details>


### [432] [ESAR: Event-Based Synthetic Aperture Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.15073)
*Harbir Antil,Daniel Blauvelt,David Sayre*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a method for monocular event-based imaging using synthetic-aperture inverse problems, enabling effective recovery of static ground-domain log-radiance fields from event cameras.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Event cameras produce asynchronous polarity events rather than conventional image frames, offering potential advantages in computational imaging, especially for dynamic scenes. However, reconstructing meaningful images from these events remains a challenging problem.

Method: The method reformulates event-based imaging as an inverse problem using synthetic-aperture techniques. It models the latent log-radiance field ($θ$) as related to motion-dependent latent images via a geometric relation. The paper applies regularized inversion techniques to recover spatial information from event data.

Result: The proposed $θ$-based formulation was tested on simulated and real Falcon Neuro event data, demonstrating improved recovery of large-scale coherent spatial structures with reduced fine-scale textures compared to prior methods.

Conclusion: A new approach for event-based imaging is introduced, which successfully recovers meaningful spatial structures by leveraging synthetic-aperture and regularized inversion techniques. This advances the ability to extract useful visual information from event camera data.

Abstract: Event cameras report asynchronous polarity events when changes in log--radiance exceed a fixed contrast threshold, producing signed temporal contrast measurements rather than conventional image frames. We formulate monocular event-based imaging as a synthetic-aperture inverse problem for a static ground-domain log--radiance field $θ\in \mathbb{R}^{N_g}$. Instead of reconstructing a latent pixel-time volume $v \in \mathbb{R}^{N_pN_t}$, we impose the geometric relation $v=Pθ$, where $P$ maps the fixed scene into motion-dependent latent views. Aggregating events over finite time intervals gives the linearized model \[
  APθ= b+η, \] where $A$ is a temporal differencing operator, $b$ contains signed binned event counts, and $η$ represents measurement and modeling errors. This decomposition exposes a synthetic-aperture structure: under near-nadir motion, successive projections are approximately shifted views of a common scene, while the composite operator $AP$ remains ill-conditioned because it combines spatial averaging with temporal differencing. We therefore use regularized inversion to recover $θ$. Numerical experiments on simulated data and real near-nadir Falcon Neuro event data show that the proposed $θ$-based formulation recovers coherent large-scale spatial structure, relative to dynamic latent-image and learned event-reconstruction baselines, while suppressing fine-scale texture.

</details>


### [433] [Domain Adaptation of Mismatched Proximal Denoiser for Plug-and-Play Image Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14894)
*Guixian Xu,Jinglai Li,Junqi Tang*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper investigates how domain mismatches affect the convergence of Plug-and-Play Proximal Gradient Descent (PnP-PGD) algorithms, introducing “proximal mismatch” as a key metric and proposing proximal matching adaptation to address the issue, with experiments showing its advantages over MSE-based adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Plug-and-Play Proximal Gradient Descent (PnP-PGD) approaches require denoisers as implicit priors, but their performance often degrades due to domain mismatches when deployed outside their training domains. The paper aims to quantify and address this issue.

Method: The authors define the concept of "proximal mismatch" to describe the discrepancy between the deployed denoiser and the reference proximal map associated with the underlying regularizer. They derive a stationarity bound that highlights the impact of proximal mismatch and propose proximal matching as an adaptation technique to minimize its effect.

Result: The analysis demonstrates that the proposed proximal matching adaptation method significantly improves reconstruction quality under domain shift, especially in Gaussian deblurring and super-resolution tasks. These improvements are most notable in the few-shot regime compared to MSE-based adaptation.

Conclusion: Proximal mismatches can significantly degrade PnP-PGD performance, but the proposed proximal matching adaptation effectively mitigates this issue. This work provides a theoretical framework and empirical evidence for improving adaptability of PnP methods in real-world scenarios.

Abstract: Plug-and-play proximal gradient descent (PnP-PGD) enables flexible image reconstruction by using denoisers as implicit priors. In practice, these denoisers are often deployed outside their training domains. Existing analyses establish convergence under structural assumptions on the deployed denoiser, such as requiring it to be a proximal map or a contraction. However, they do not measure how domain mismatch affects convergence of PnP-PGD. We define this effect as \emph{proximal mismatch}: the discrepancy between a deployed denoiser $\widehat{\mathsf D}$ and a target-domain reference map $\mathsf D_\star=\operatorname{prox}_{R_\star}$ associated with the underlying regularizer $R_\star$. Under this mismatch, each denoising update becomes an inexact proximal step for the target objective. We further derive a stationarity bound that decays at a rate of $\mathcal{O}(1/K)$, with an additive term proportional to the average squared proximal mismatch. This result motivates adaptation via proximal matching rather than MSE-based adaptation alone. We study this approach with two established denoiser families: learned proximal networks and gradient-step denoisers. Experiments on Gaussian deblurring and super-resolution under substantial domain shift show that proximal matching adaptation improves reconstruction quality significantly over MSE-based adaptation, yielding the largest numerical gains in the few-shot regime.

</details>


<div id='cs.IR'></div>

# cs.IR [[Back]](#toc)

### [434] [Does generative AI supersede supervised XMLC? A Benchmark Study on Automated Subject Indexing with German Scientific Literature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14882)
*Maximilian Kähler,Katja Konermann,Lisa Kluge,Markus Schumacher*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: This paper examines methods for automating subject indexing in libraries, using Extreme Multi-Label Classification (XMLC) techniques, and compares their effectiveness with traditional lexical approaches and LLM-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve automated subject indexing for large controlled vocabularies in library systems, particularly focusing on contemporary German literature indexed by the German National Library, addressing existing challenges in handling long-tail subject terms.

Method: The study applies specialized supervised XMLC methods, traditional lexical matching, and custom-developed LLM-based methods. These methods are benchmarked using binary relevance and graded relevance ratings, including assessments from professional librarians.

Result: Transformer-based XMLC methods outperform others in general binary relevance metrics, but LLM-based generative methods excel in graded relevance and handling long-tail vocabulary terms.

Conclusion: While XMLC approaches are strong overall, LLM-based generative methods demonstrate superior performance in handling niche and graded relevance cases, suggesting they are a promising avenue for future research and application.

Abstract: With a large controlled vocabulary as the label set, the task of automated subject indexing in a library can be understood as a multi-label classification task. If the set of subject terms is large, the problem fits the Extreme Multi-Label Classification (XMLC) objective. In this study, we apply a selection of specialised supervised XMLC methods to the test case of subject indexing contemporary German scientific literature, collected at the German National Library (DNB). We contrast these results by including a classical lexical matching baseline and three of our own recently developed LLM-based methods into the benchmark. Algorithms are evaluated and compared in several metrics. This includes binary relevance comparisons with previously indexed material, as well as graded relevance ratings by professional subject librarians. A challenge for all methods is to reliably make suggestions from the long tail of the subject vocabulary. We find that supervised XMLC algorithms relying on transformer-based dense features give best results in terms of overall binary relevance metrics. However, focusing on graded relevance and performance in the long tail of our subject vocabulary, the LLM-based generative methods give better results, making them a promising alternative for future productive use.

</details>


### [435] [Deep-learning Causal Retrieval Optimization for Efficient e-commerce Distribution in Pinterest](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14161)
*Junpeng Hou,XianXing Zhang,Sai Xiao,Derek Cheng,Darren Reger,Olafur Gudmundsson,Mehdi Ben Ayed,Zhiqing Rao,Huizhong Duan*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces a system for improving shopping content delivery on Pinterest by utilizing deep causal learning and personalized triggering policies in early content retrieval. Results showcase significant reduction in shopping triggers and enhancements in user engagement metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure shopping content on Pinterest aids user exploration rather than distracting, by optimizing when and how it should be presented.

Method: A deep multi-task model is developed, leveraging causal inference and personalized triggering policies. It uses doubly-robust pseudo-outcome training, calibrated losses, randomized data logging, and offline replay for optimization.

Result: Shopping triggers are reduced by up to 85%, with improvements in key engagement metrics like session retention (+0.26%) and Pin saves (+1.10%). Infrastructure demands are significantly lowered.

Conclusion: The study successfully demonstrates a scalable and practical approach for balancing commerce content delivery, ensuring content relevance and improving user experience in cascading recommenders.

Abstract: Pinterest is where people turn inspiration into action as users browse ideas, then take steps toward realization, often by discovering shoppable content. To support this journey, we must distribute commerce content when it helps, not when it distracts. We frame this as a causal decision of triggering shopping candidate generators in early retrieval and deploy a production system at Pinterest that learns personalized and contextualized triggering policies. A deep multi-task model jointly predicts outcomes and uplift of multiple events, trained with a doubly-robust pseudo-outcome alongside calibrated outcome losses for stable, single-robust uplift learning. A randomized data logging supplies counterfactual coverage, and the model is evaluated by both regular and reverse metrics for full assessment. A linear-time offline replay is designed to select thresholds and forecast policy impact with extremely high consistency with online results. For productionization, the model runs in parallel with remote retrieval calls without end-to-end latency regression. At web scale, we cut shopping triggers by up to 85% while holding key shopping sessions neutral, improving important total sessions (+0.26%) and Pin saves (+1.10%), with significant infrastructure savings. By unifying deep causal learning with reliable offline replay and demonstrating production-grade deployment, this work provides a generally practical recipe for early-retrieval optimizations in modern cascading recommenders beyond shopping, aligning exploration and cost with user intent at scale.

</details>


### [436] [Bridge Evidence: Static Retrieval Utility Does Not Predict Causal Utility in Multi-Step Agentic Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.15253)
*Debayan Mukhopadhyay,Utshab Kumar Ghosh,Shubham Chatterjee*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper argues that traditional retrieval system evaluation methods are insufficient for language models acting as search agents, proposing a new metric called Counterfactual Trajectory Utility (CTU).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in current retrieval systems that fail to account for how documents contribute to future query generation and overall search trajectory in agentic retrieval models.

Method: Use a ReAct-style agent and HotpotQA dataset to replay 1000 development questions, deleting individual documents and analyzing the changes in answer quality, query retrieval, and turn count to derive a Counterfactual Trajectory Utility (CTU) score.

Result: The study finds that traditional relevance scores and causal usefulness are largely independent, with roughly one-third of documents providing critical utility ("bridge documents") despite appearing irrelevant in static evaluations. The pattern remains consistent across different relevance scoring proxies.

Conclusion: Optimizing static relevance in retrieval systems does not guarantee causal usefulness in agentic retrieval. A new focus on metrics like CTU is needed to understand and optimize retrieval systems for language model agents.

Abstract: Retrieval systems are trained and evaluated on a static idea of usefulness: hand a document and a question to a reader model, see whether the answer improves, and score the document accordingly. The idea holds up when a document is read on its own. It breaks when a language model works as a search agent, issuing several queries and reasoning across turns, because a document can matter for what it lets the agent do next rather than for what it says about the current question.
  We measure that gap rather than argue it. Using a ReAct style agent over HotpotQA, we replay 1000 development questions and, for every document the agent read, delete it and re-run the rest of the trajectory from that point. Comparing the original run against its counterfactual gives a Counterfactual Trajectory Utility (CTU) score from three deltas: final answer quality, next query retrieval quality, and turn count. Crossing CTU against Static RAG Utility (SRU) over 23,322 document observations, the two are close to statistically independent (Spearman rho = -0.026). Roughly a third of the documents the agent reads are causally load bearing while looking useless to a static reader; we call these bridge documents. The pattern survives when the reader based axis is swapped for a BM25 and cross encoder proxy, giving a bridge cell of 27.2% on an evenly spread axis.
  A second experiment pins down the mechanism. Using the Observable Entity Relevance (OER) measure from prior work, entities that discriminate relevant from non-relevant candidates appear in the agent's next query 4.02 times more often than entities found only in non-relevant documents (6.1% vs 1.5%, n = 227,139). A bridge document earns its keep by handing the agent a discriminative entity that redirects the search. Static relevance and causal usefulness are different quantities in agentic retrieval, and optimizing the first does not deliver the second.

</details>


<div id='cs.CY'></div>

# cs.CY [[Back]](#toc)

### [437] [Penny: Transition Network Analysis of Learner-Chatbot Interactions in Scaffolded EFL Writing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14575)
*Steve Woollaston,Brendan Flanagan,Yuko Toyokawa,Hiroaki Ogata*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The study examines how Japanese EFL learners use an AI writing chatbot, revealing different engagement patterns based on proficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the pedagogical value and interaction dynamics of AI chatbots in improving EFL writing skills.

Method: Transition Network Analysis was applied to over 4,500 writing sessions and 21,000 chatbot interactions to identify behavioral patterns.

Result: Two behavioral loops were identified: "Revision Loop" focusing on error correction and "Chat Loop" engaging learners in sustained dialogue. Learner proficiency influenced these patterns.

Conclusion: Differentiated chatbot designs are needed to foster deeper cognitive engagement, beyond error correction, suited to diverse learner levels.

Abstract: Generative AI chatbots promise to transform English as a Foreign Language (EFL) writing by providing immediate, personalised feedback. However, their pedagogical value depends on how learners engage with them - a process often treated as a "black box." This study uses Transition Network Analysis to model the temporal dynamics of Japanese EFL learners using "Penny," an LLM-powered writing chatbot. Analysis of over 4,500 writing sessions and 21,000 chatbot interactions reveals two dominant behavioural loops: a "Revision Loop," where feedback leads directly to successful error correction, and a "Chat Loop," where learners engage in sustained dialogue with the chatbot following feedback. Crucially, EFL proficiency significantly shapes interaction: high-proficiency learners engage more in open dialogue and negotiation with the chatbot, while low-proficiency learners rely more heavily on repetitive corrective feedback cycles. The findings demonstrate that AI-scaffolded writing is a non-linear, dialogic process and highlight the need for differentiated chatbot design to move beyond simple error correction and foster deeper cognitive engagement for all learners.

</details>


### [438] [Unsafe at any AUC: Unlearned Lessons from Sociotechnical Disasters for Responsible AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14353)
*Joshua A. Kroll,Andrew Smart,R. Stuart Geiger,Abigail Z. Jacobs*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper discusses the importance of sociotechnical analysis for understanding and mitigating risks in AI systems, learning from past catastrophic failures in highly complex systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation lies in addressing risks associated with automated decision-making and data-driven technologies by analyzing them within sociotechnical systems instead of just focusing on their technical components.

Method: The authors study historical human-made catastrophes, draw lessons unlearned in their risk management, and apply these lessons to AI systems, proposing improvements in risk perception, communication, and responsibility allocation.

Result: The paper identifies critical areas for improvement in AI risk management by transferring lessons from other domains, focusing on organizational dynamics and sociotechnical frameworks.

Conclusion: AI systems need a broader approach to safety and responsibility, incorporating social and organizational factors into their design and evaluation to prevent systemic failures.

Abstract: As automated decision-making and data-driven technologies pervade society and are used to manage consequential outcomes, understanding the technology's capabilities, limitations, and attendant risks in context requires analysis of full sociotechnical systems. Sociotechnical analysis of risks in highly complex systems provides clear lessons for the design and evaluation of AI systems, transcending a technical focus on reliable or "responsibly designed" components to understand risks at a systems level.
  Human-made catastrophes have been studied for decades because of the severity of these events: consider Chernobyl, Three Mile Island, Fukushima-Daiichi, Bhopal, the Challenger disaster. A common misconception is that these kinds of events are freak accidents, resulting from the inherently unforeseeable interactions in complex systems. Closer examination reveals that the risks and hazards were well-known beforehand but not acted upon due to social structural, political and economic factors.
  We outline several areas where the development and use of AI can benefit from learning these unlearned lessons: improved risk perception, communication, and analysis at the organizational level; traceability of requirements and responsibilities; and holistic approaches to responsibility and safety that include social and organizational dynamics as first-order engineering concerns. For each area, we offer concrete unlearned lessons and exemplify how they led to failure in prior accidents as well as examples of how these lessons remain unlearned for modern computing systems, particularly AI.

</details>


<div id='cs.ET'></div>

# cs.ET [[Back]](#toc)

### [439] [Global drivers and barriers to the public acceptance of autonomous vehicles: Evidence from 17 countries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14436)
*Antonios Saravanos*

Main category: cs.ET

TL;DR: The study explores global public acceptance of Level 3 automated cars, emphasizing factors like usefulness, social support, and enjoyment over demographics or ease-of-use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address a knowledge gap about global factors influencing public acceptance of Level 3 conditionally automated cars, given that prior research primarily focused on European samples.

Method: A UTAUT2-based structural equation model was applied to survey data from 18,603 respondents across 17 countries to evaluate how performance expectancy, effort expectancy, social influence, facilitating conditions, and hedonic motivation impact acceptance.

Result: Performance expectancy, social influence, and hedonic motivation were the primary drivers of intention to use Level 3 cars, with smaller contributions from effort expectancy and facilitating conditions. Demographics and experience had weaker but statistically significant effects.

Conclusion: Acceptance of Level 3 automated cars globally is less influenced by demographics or ease-of-use concerns and more by perceptions of usefulness, social support, and enjoyment.

Abstract: This study investigated the public acceptance of Society of Automotive Engineers Level 3 conditionally automated cars, which can self-drive under certain specified conditions but require the human driver to remain ready to resume control when requested. Previous Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology 2 (UTAUT2)-based research has focused mainly on European samples, and so it is still unclear whether the same factors shape acceptance across broader world regions. This knowledge gap was addressed using the L3Pilot Global User Acceptance Survey. From an original dataset of 18,631 respondents, the final analytic sample comprised 18,603 respondents from 17 countries across Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, and South America. The data were analyzed using a UTAUT2-based structural equation model to examine how performance expectancy, effort expectancy, social influence, facilitating conditions, and hedonic motivation shape the intention to use Level 3 cars. The model showed strong explanatory power. Across the analytic sample, the intention to use Level 3 cars was driven mainly by performance expectancy, social influence, and hedonic motivation. Effort expectancy and facilitating conditions also contributed, but they played smaller direct roles. Age, gender, and previous experience with advanced driver assistance systems were statistically significant, but comparatively weak predictors. Overall, the findings suggest that the acceptance of Level 3 automated cars depends less on demographic characteristics or ease-of-use concerns and more on whether people see the technology as useful, socially supported, and enjoyable to use.

</details>


<div id='quant-ph'></div>

# quant-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [440] [Real-Time Detection of Charge Jumps in Superconducting Qubits with a Convolutional Neural Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14293)
*Daniel Gaytan-Villarreal,Peter Meiring,Daniel Baxter,Daniel Bowring,Grace Bratrud,Matteo Cremonesi,Giuseppe Di Guglielmo,Grace Wagner,Bowen Xiao*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces an online detector using a dilated causal convolutional neural network (DCCNN) for charge jumps in superconducting qubits, enabling real-time fault-tolerant quantum computing and quantum sensing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Charge jumps induced by cosmic rays and gamma radiation challenge fault-tolerant quantum computing and provide opportunities for quantum sensing, but current detection methods are too slow for real-time control.

Method: The authors propose a DCCNN implemented on the Quantum Instrumentation Control Kit (QICK) platform, trained on synthetic data from the NEXUS experiment and converted into FPGA firmware for low-latency operation.

Result: The DCCNN achieves similar detection efficiency to an established offline method ($0.843 ␀b 0.022$ vs. $0.866 ␀b 0.020$) while operating entirely online with no hyperparameter tuning.

Conclusion: This approach enables real-time charge-jump detection for quantum error mitigation and particle detection, moving it from post-hoc diagnostics to adaptive control-loop integration.

Abstract: Ionizing radiation from cosmic rays and gammas can induce discontinuous jumps in the environmental charge of superconducting qubits (charge jumps), causing correlated errors that challenge fault-tolerant quantum computing while simultaneously providing a detection signature for quantum sensing applications. Current detection methods operate offline, introducing latency incompatible with in-the-loop qubit control. In this paper, an online detector of charge jumps for superconducting qubits, based on a dilated causal convolutional neural network (DCCNN) designed for in-the-loop deployment on the Quantum Instrumentation Control Kit (QICK) platform, is presented. The network is trained on synthetic Ramsey tomography scans generated from qubit templates measured at the Northwestern Experimental Underground Site (NEXUS) at Fermilab, and translated to FPGA firmware via hls4ml with ap_fixed$\langle 16,6 \rangle$ quantization, reaching a per-inference latency of $6.19 μ$s on the Zynq UltraScale+ RFSoC ZCU216. At this operating point the DCCNN matches the detection efficiency of the established offline $χ^2$ algorithm ($0.843 \pm 0.022$ vs. $0.866 \pm 0.020$ on $|Δq| \in [0.1, 0.5] e$ at matched false-positive rate), while requiring no per-qubit hyperparameter tuning. This shifts charge-jump detection from a post-hoc diagnostic to a control-loop primitive, enabling adaptive protocols that respond to radiation-induced events in situ, with applications to quantum-computing error mitigation and to the use of superconducting qubits as particle detectors.

</details>


<div id='stat.AP'></div>

# stat.AP [[Back]](#toc)

### [441] [Analysis of Public Schools Educational Performance Based on Causal Models and Hierarchical Clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14124)
*Anderson L. de Paula,Pedro C. dos Santos,Renato A. Krohling*

Main category: stat.AP

TL;DR: The study analyzes large-scale educational data in Brazil, identifies school typologies through clustering, and examines their link to educational outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges posed by institutional heterogeneity and structural complexity in educational data and to better understand the factors influencing school performance.

Method: Data preprocessing, normalization, hierarchical clustering to identify school typologies, and causal analysis techniques for studying relationships with educational outcomes.

Result: The identification of distinct school profiles, statistically significant differences in average performance among these profiles, and insights into structural/contextual factors influencing education.

Conclusion: The findings enhance understanding of factors associated with school effectiveness and provide a framework for analyzing complex educational data.

Abstract: The increasing availability of large-scale educational datasets has expanded the use of quantitative methods for investigating school performance. However, institutional heterogeneity among schools and the structural complexity of educational data pose substantial challenges to traditional statistical modeling approaches. This study investigates the existence of school typologies based on structural, pedagogical, and demographic characteristics, and examines how these typologies relate to performance in the Brazilian Basic Education Assessment System (Saeb). Using data from the Brazilian School Census and Saeb, data preprocessing and normalization procedures are applied followed by hierarchical clustering to identify groups of schools with similar structural profiles. After the identification of these typologies, causal analysis techniques are employed to investigate potential causal relationships between school characteristics and educational outcomes. The results reveal the presence of distinct school profiles and statistically significant differences in average performance among them. The causal analysis provides insights into the structural and contextual factors that may influence educational performance, contributing to a better understanding of the mechanisms associated with school effectiveness.

</details>


<div id='econ.GN'></div>

# econ.GN [[Back]](#toc)

### [442] [Does Multi-Agent Debate Improve AI Feedback on Research Papers?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14713)
*Tomas Havranek,Zuzana Irsova*

Main category: econ.GN

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether AI can effectively support authors of meta-analyses in economics by comparing a single-pass AI model to two multi-agent debate AI tools for generating review reports.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to understand the usefulness of AI tools in assisting authors of meta-analyses, addressing whether such tools can replace traditional journal referee reports in certain aspects.

Method: A pre-registered, identity-masked, within-paper experiment was conducted with 44 meta-analysis authors evaluating their own work. They ranked three AI-generated reports (one single-pass model and two multi-agent debate tools) based on usefulness, using a standard length and template.

Result: Authors preferred the single-pass AI model over the multi-agent debate tools, with a statistically significant preference. However, authors still rated traditional journal referee reports higher overall. Notably, when evaluated by an AI judge, rankings differed from the human authors’ preferences.

Conclusion: AI reports, particularly single-pass models, show promise in assisting authors but cannot fully replace human referees. The judgment of AI models differs from human preferences, cautioning against substituting AI evaluations for human insights.

Abstract: Probably not, at least for meta-analyses in economics. In a pre-registered, identity-masked, within-paper experiment, the authors of 44 meta-analyses ranked three AI reports on their own paper by usefulness for improving it: a single pass by a frontier model against two multi-agent debate tools we built and expected to win. All reports were held to a common length and template. The authors preferred the single pass, by 0.66 rank points over mad-research (95% CI 0.32 to 1.00) and 0.57 over paper-workshop (0.16 to 0.95), though paper-workshop spent roughly thirty times the tokens. Authors who recalled their journal referee report usually placed it first and never last; in a separate exercise, three AI judges almost always placed the real journal referee report last. Among the three AI reports, Gemini (the judge whose model family wrote none of the reports) would have ranked paper-workshop first in the authors' place, reversing the single-pass preference. The reversal warns against substituting an AI judge for the author. We measure perceived usefulness for finished papers; whether AI should referee papers is a separate question.

</details>


### [443] [Governing Artificial Intelligence: Public Preferences and Regulatory Options](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14585)
*Magnus Lundgren,Jonas Tallberg*

Main category: econ.GN

TL;DR: Citizens generally prioritize safety, public regulation, and international governance for AI, contrasting dominant regulatory trends.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand citizen preferences regarding key choices in AI regulation, given its transformative societal impact.

Method: A conjoint survey experiment across seven countries with diverse political and economic backgrounds.

Result: Global citizens strongly support AI regulation, favoring safety, public oversight, and international approaches. Preferences are influenced by individual risk perceptions of AI.

Conclusion: There is a clear misalignment between current regulatory frameworks for AI and public opinion, indicating the need to align future policies.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly transforming economies, societies, and polities, raising fundamental questions about how it should be regulated. Policymakers face choices over whether to prioritize innovation or safety, rely on public oversight or private self-regulation, and govern nationally or internationally. Yet little is known about how citizens evaluate these competing priorities. Here we report a conjoint survey experiment conducted in seven countries with diverse political and economic profiles. We find that citizens strongly support regulating AI and generally prioritize safety over innovation, public governance over private self-regulation, and international over national approaches. The preference for safety is strongest among those who perceive AI as risky, unpredictable, and personally consequential. These findings reveal a systematic misalignment between dominant regulatory approaches and citizen preferences.

</details>


<div id='math.ST'></div>

# math.ST [[Back]](#toc)

### [444] [Precise sample covariance spectral norm error -- an RDT view](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14460)
*Mihailo Stojnic*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: This paper analyzes the sample covariance error of centered Gaussian distributions, deriving precise limits for its spectral norm using Random Duality Theory (RDT).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study builds on prior work that established the error scaling order of covariance matrices to explicitly determine the limiting spectral norm error, which is critical for applications in high-dimensional statistics.

Method: The authors developed a framework rooted in Random Duality Theory (RDT), derived explicit upper bounds, and combined a novel lower-bounding mechanism with a two-replica systems strategy to achieve matching bounds in high dimensions.

Result: Closed-form bounds for the covariance error's spectral norm are derived and demonstrated to match in large sample dimensions. Numerical simulations confirm the accuracy even for problem sizes in the thousands.

Conclusion: This work improves analytical understanding of covariance matrix error by providing precise spectral norm limits and validating them through theoretical and numerical evidence.

Abstract: We study the sample covariance error of centered Gaussians. A remarkable breakthrough [66] established the correct error scaling order and explicitly revealed the critical role of both the effective rank and the true covariance spectrum.
  In this work, we move beyond scaling characterizations and determine the precise limiting value of the error's spectral norm. To do so, we develop a generic framework based on Random Duality Theory (RDT). Within this framework, we first determine closed-form, explicit RDT-based upper bounds. We then establish complementary lower bounds by introducing a novel bilinear-quadratic RDT lower-bounding mechanism. By combining this mechanism with a two-replica systems bounding strategy, we show that our lower and upper bounds match in large-dimensional contexts. Our theoretical results are supplemented with numerical evaluations and simulations, demonstrating an excellent agreement already for problem sizes on the order of thousands.

</details>


### [445] [Measuring Spatial Clustering via Metropolis-Hastings Diffusion Distance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14880)
*Thomas Weighill,Chidinma Williams*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: The paper introduces the diffusion distance, a novel metric for measuring the discrepancy between two probability distributions on a graph, which extends Moran's I by leveraging global graph geometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in existing measures of spatial autocorrelation, such as Moran's I, by incorporating global graph geometry rather than focusing solely on local patterns.

Method: The authors propose a diffusion distance based on the rate of convergence of a Markov chain targeting a stationary distribution. They derive theoretical bounds, stability results, and statistical tests for spatial clustering based on this measure.

Result: Diffusion distance outperforms Moran's I in detecting spatial clustering, demonstrating higher power on synthetic datasets and identifying nuanced urban segregation patterns in real-world data from 100 U.S. cities.

Conclusion: Diffusion distance provides a more robust and sensitive framework for analyzing spatial clustering by leveraging global graph structures, making it superior to traditional measures like Moran's I in specific contexts.

Abstract: We propose a novel measure of the discrepancy between two probability distributions $f$ and $g$ on a graph - which we call the diffusion distance - that measures the rate of convergence of $f$ to $g$ under a graph-constrained Markov chain with stationary distribution $g$. As a default choice for this Markov chain, we use the Metropolis-Hastings transition matrix targeting $g$ with proposals given by a random walk on the graph. Our primary case of interest is when the second distribution $g$ is uniform, in which case the diffusion distance becomes a measure of spatial clustering in $f$. Used in this way, (Metropolis-Hastings) diffusion distance to uniformity extends Moran's $I$-type measures of spatial autocorrelation by incorporating global graph geometry rather than just local patterns. Indeed, Moran's $I$, the most well-known measure of spatial autocorrelation, can be viewed as a one-step heuristic for diffusion distance, so long as specific spatial weights are used. We establish theoretical bounds and a stability result for our measure, connecting it to graph spectra and optimal transport. We then turn our attention to outlining a statistical test for spatial clustering using diffusion distance. Under permutation null models, we derive high-probability bounds on diffusion distance underpinned by exact spectral formulas for convergence of distributions, enabling an efficient statistical test for spatial clustering on large datasets. We empirically compare diffusion distance to Moran's $I$ both as a numerical measure and as a statistical test. We show that diffusion distance exhibits higher power on synthetic data using a stochastic block model. Empirical analysis of Black population distributions for 100 U.S. cities shows that diffusion distance detects subtle differences in urban segregation patterns that Moran's $I$ does not.

</details>


<div id='cs.LO'></div>

# cs.LO [[Back]](#toc)

### [446] [Can LLMs Build a MaxSAT Solver from Papers? The CoreForge Experience](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14818)
*Ruben Martins*

Main category: cs.LO

TL;DR: The paper explores using large language models (LLMs) to develop a MaxSAT solver directly from research papers, rather than preexisting codebases, and evaluates its effectiveness and challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to investigate whether large language models can assist in the development of advanced algorithmic solvers like MaxSAT by interpreting and implementing algorithms detailed in academic papers.

Method: The authors used an iterative workflow combining ChatGPT and Codex for interpreting papers, writing code, revising implementations, and performing LLM-assisted audits and benchmarking.

Result: The LLM-built solver implemented a variety of MaxSAT functionalities like core-guided optimization and lightweight preprocessing. While performance lagged behind state-of-the-art solvers, the configurations tested did not yield wrong answers.

Conclusion: LLMs show promise in aiding the development of solvers from research papers, but still require significant human oversight, validation, and optimization to match hand-engineered solutions.

Abstract: We report on CoreForge, an experience in using large language models (LLMs) to build an unweighted MaxSAT solver from research papers rather than from an existing solver codebase. The project focuses on unsatisfiability-based MaxSAT algorithms and follows an iterative workflow that combines paper discussions with ChatGPT, implementation through Codex prompts, and repeated LLM-assisted code audits and revisions. Although the codebase implements several algorithms and solver components, our evaluation focuses on configurations that combine core-guided optimization, lightweight preprocessing, core minimization, integration with integer linear optimization backends, and a new core-sequence lookahead approach.
  Our experience suggests that LLMs can support solver implementation from papers, while requiring external validation, benchmarking, and human guidance. In our experiments, fuzzing and MaxSAT Evaluation instances did not reveal wrong answers in the tested configurations, although performance remains below the best hand-engineered MaxSAT solvers. We summarize what worked, what remained difficult, and the lessons for future LLM-assisted solver development.

</details>


<div id='cs.CR'></div>

# cs.CR [[Back]](#toc)

### [447] [A Measurement Study of AI-Environment Realism Gaps in Malware-Analysis Sandboxes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14434)
*Zhiyong Sui,Lamine Noureddine,Mst Eshita Khatun,Sideeq Bello,Babangida Bappah,Justin Woodring,Aisha Ali-Gombe*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces AIprint, a framework to identify AI-environment artifacts as a new sandbox-evasion method used by malware, demonstrating gaps in current sandbox detection techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Malware increasingly suppresses execution when analysis is suspected, exploiting the realism gaps in sandbox environments and targeting AI-environment artifacts.

Method: AIprint probes over 450 artifacts from 284 AI projects, evaluates them against sandboxes and reference AI-capable hosts, conducts installation experiments, and tests adaptive spoofing costs.

Result: Twelve unique AI artifacts consistently differentiate AI-capable systems from sandbox backends, highlighting significant gaps in traditional VM-detection methods.

Conclusion: AI-environment artifacts present a powerful sandbox-evasion surface. Detecting them is easier than convincingly mimicking AI ecosystems, posing challenges for sandbox improvements.

Abstract: Sandboxing remains a core technique for observing suspicious program behavior, yet environment-aware malware increasingly suppresses execution when analysis is suspected. Prior generations of sandbox evasion focused on virtualization artifacts, timing discrepancies, and wear-and-tear realism. In this paper, we present the first systematic measurement study of AI-environment artifacts as a new sandbox-evasion surface. We operationalize this realism gap through AIprint, a probe framework that captures persistent artifacts left behind by AI-capable software ecosystems, including AI-assistant configuration directories, model caches, environment variables, local inference services, and package dependencies.
  We systematically extract 450 unique artifacts from 284 open-source AI projects on GitHub, compile them into unprivileged Windows probes, and evaluate them across seven commercial and open-source sandbox backends together with three AI-capable reference hosts. Our results show that traditional VM-detection baselines fail to reliably distinguish real AI-capable systems from modern sandboxes, whereas twelve AI-environment artifacts appear on the reference hosts and on none of the evaluated backends. A controlled 214-step installation experiment establishes a causal relationship between AI tool and package installation and measurable AI-environment artifact accumulation, while adaptive spoofing experiments reveal a fundamental operational asymmetry: reproducing convincing AI software environments is substantially more expensive than detecting shallow spoofing.

</details>


### [448] [The Distributed Open-Source Vulnerability Ecosystem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14900)
*Peter Mandl,Paul Mandl*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper addresses inconsistencies in vulnerability scanner outputs by proposing a conceptual framework that analyzes the open-source vulnerability ecosystem from creation to interpretation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to tackle inconsistencies in reported software vulnerabilities by scanners, which undermine security management in the software supply chain.

Method: The paper introduces a conceptual framework that traces vulnerability information across stages like creation, standardization, enrichment, and interpretation.

Result: The analysis identifies sources such as heterogeneous data, versioning models, and temporal changes as key causes of inconsistencies in scanner results.

Conclusion: Implications for improving scanner reliability involve emphasizing reproducible methods and better handling of dynamic vulnerability knowledge.

Abstract: Identifying known software vulnerabilities is a central task in software supply chain security management. Although publicly available vulnerability information is based on shared standards, different vulnerability scanners often report divergent results for identical software inventories. These differences do not arise solely from individual data sources or scanner implementations. They can emerge at several stages of the open-source vulnerability ecosystem. This paper presents a conceptual framework that describes vulnerability management as a distributed process of information exchange and transformation. It traces vulnerability information from its creation and standardization through enrichment to context-dependent interpretation. The analysis identifies heterogeneous information sources, divergent identity and version models, temporal change, and context-dependent assessment as major causes of inconsistent scanner findings. It then discusses the implications for interpreting analysis results, designing reproducible evaluation methods, and handling dynamic vulnerability knowledge in practice.

</details>


### [449] [Setup Complete, Now You Are Compromised: Weaponizing Setup Instructions Against AI Coding Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.15143)
*Aadesh Bagmar,Pushkar Saraf*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper analyzes AI coding agents' vulnerability to supply-chain attacks through project documentation, unveiling significant security blind spots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Attacks exploiting AI coding agents via manipulated project documentation pose major security risks, requiring analysis and countermeasures.

Method: The authors systematically evaluated twelve scenarios across five attack types on several coding-agent models, focusing on their responses to security threats in project setup.

Result: Agents catch typosquats often but fail at subtle attacks like separator-confusion or source-based vulnerabilities, showing inconsistent security across tools and ecosystems.

Conclusion: Prompt engineering mitigates some issues but can't fully address the security gap; a deterministic pre-install check is crucial for improving install-time safety.

Abstract: AI coding agents set up projects by reading documentation and installing the dependencies it lists, without verifying their names, sources, or known vulnerabilities. By editing only a README, requirements file, or Makefile, an attacker can redirect the agent to an untrusted registry, a known-vulnerable version, or a wrong-but-plausible name: documentation becomes a vector for code execution. We present the first systematic evaluation of package-install-time supply-chain attacks delivered through ordinary project-setup documentation across production coding-agent harnesses, probing frontier models on twelve scenarios in five attack classes, grounded in documented incidents. The same model catches an attack through one harness and installs it through another: install-time security rests on the harness-model combination, not the model alone. Agents catch blatant typosquats reliably, but plausible separator-confusion names (azurecore for azure-core) slip through, and how often depends on the harness-model pairing. Source-based attacks like registry redirection are missed almost everywhere. The source blind spot recurs on npm and Cargo, where nearly every model installs the untrusted dependency; name detection carries over less consistently across ecosystems. Security-oriented prompts recover part of the gap but only for the dimension they name; a deterministic pre-install check that verifies names, sources, and versions before any code runs closes most of it.

</details>


### [450] [Qubes OS Security in the Public Record](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14587)
*Alfonso De Gregorio*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper analyzes 109 Qubes Security Bulletins (QSBs) and other vulnerability data to evaluate the security reporting of Qubes OS, focusing on upstream dependencies and disclosure trends.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to investigate the security advisory ecosystem of Qubes OS, a system where component boundaries are critical to security, by analyzing its public records to reveal trends and dependencies.

Method: A protocol-driven approach combining deterministic attribution, statistical analyses (e.g., change-point, overdispersion), and vulnerability discovery model evaluation to study vulnerability disclosures and their origins.

Result: The analysis identifies upstream dependencies as a primary burden (79.8% of QSBs linked to Xen, CPU, or upstream issues), reveals a significant disclosure pattern change in 2015Q1, and finds stable disclosure rates post-2018. However, vulnerability discovery models did not outperform baselines in forecasting.

Conclusion: Qubes OS's security advisory trends are stable in volume but still exhibit a significant reliance on upstream components. Despite plateaued disclosure rates, upstream dependencies remain critical, highlighting the need for robust upstream security management.

Abstract: Qubes OS is a revealing case for security measurement because its architecture makes component boundaries security-relevant. We present a protocol-driven longitudinal analysis of 109 public Qubes Security Bulletins (QSBs, 2011--2025), the official Qubes-maintained Xen Security Advisory (XSA) tracker, and a secondary vulnerability-event sensitivity series. The study measures the public advisory record rather than latent vulnerability incidence or realized compromise. The methodology combines audited deterministic component attribution, change-point analysis, overdispersion checks, severity-proxy weighting, censoring sensitivity, documentary latency lower bounds, and baseline-aware evaluation of vulnerability discovery models (VDMs).
  The results show persistent upstream dependence in that public record. On the official tracker, 113 of 464 XSAs affect Qubes; under primary labeling, 87 of 109 QSBs (79.8\%) are attributable to Xen, CPU/microarchitectural, or other upstream components rather than Qubes-core logic, with similar results under weighted views. Change-point analyses identify 2015Q1 as the dominant break in the quarterly advisory series, while post-2018 annual disclosure rates are statistically flat. Poisson inferences are stable under dispersion diagnostics and negative-binomial sensitivity checks. The attribution codebook performs well in a stratified 30-QSB audit, and S-shaped VDMs fit descriptively but do not significantly outperform a rolling-mean baseline in short-horizon forecasts.
  Overall, the Qubes public advisory record appears stable, but not quiet: disclosure activity plateaus at a higher level than in the earliest years, while the observed burden remains concentrated in upstream trust anchors.

</details>


### [451] [Bad Memory: Evaluating Prompt Injection Risks from Memory in Agentic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14611)
*Soham Gadgil,David Alexander,Sai Sunku,Franziska Roesner*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper investigates prompt injection attacks in memory-based agentic systems, highlighting vulnerabilities in persistent memory that can influence future behavior and sessions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the security risks posed by prompt injection attacks targeting the persistent memory and adaptation features of agentic systems, which are increasingly used but create new vulnerabilities.

Method: The researchers used a sandboxed synthetic workspace to evaluate the susceptibility of two agentic systems (Anthropic Claude Code and OpenAI Codex) across four models (Claude Haiku 4.5, Claude Opus 4.7, GPT-5.2, and GPT-5.5) to prompt injection attacks, focusing on multi-session attack sequences.

Result: It is challenging to make an agent overwrite its memory from untrusted external contents, but pre-planted malicious payloads in memory files can attack future sessions successfully. Success rates and persistence of such attacks significantly vary depending on multiple factors including system, model, and attack scenarios.

Conclusion: Persistent memory introduces new attack surfaces for prompt injection, necessitating advanced defenses that secure memory updates without hindering agent adaptability.

Abstract: A growing class of agentic systems maintain persistent state across sessions through memory files, behavioral preferences, and knowledge bases. While this makes agents more useful and self-improving, it also creates a new attack surface for prompt injections in which malicious instructions can be embedded within persistent files and influence future behavior. In this work, we study prompt injection attacks in memory-based agentic systems using a sandboxed synthetic workspace. We evaluate two agentic systems, Anthropic Claude Code and OpenAI Codex, across four models: Claude Haiku 4.5, Claude Opus 4.7, GPT-5.2, and GPT-5.5. Our results show that although it is difficult to make an agent overwrite its own memory files using untrusted external content, payloads already planted in those files can successfully attack current and future sessions. Attack success and payload persistence vary substantially across systems, models, adversarial goals, and multi-session attack sequences. These findings show that persistent memory changes the threat model for prompt injection and motivate defenses that protect memory updates without removing useful agent adaptation.

</details>


### [452] [MemPoison: Uncovering Persistent Memory Threats and Structural Blind Spots in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14651)
*Jifeng Gao,Kang Xia,Yi Zhang,Xiaobin Hong,Mingkai Lin,Xingshen Wei,Wenzhong Li,Sanglu Lu*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper explores security risks in persistent external memory for agents, unveiling attack typologies, benchmarks, and defense gaps, advocating for adaptive memory defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address security vulnerabilities introduced by persistent external memories in interactive agents.

Method: The paper introduces MemPoison, a benchmark with 1227 validated cases to analyze attack types, injection channels, and memory substrates, alongside evaluations using MID.

Result: Identified vulnerabilities in baseline defenses against L2 and L3 attacks while highlighting structural blind spots in write-time strategies.

Conclusion: Suggests transitioning from static filtering to dynamic, context-aware defense systems for better security in persistent memory.

Abstract: Persistent external memory enhances agent continuity but introduces persistent security vulnerabilities: adversarial content can be injected via standard interaction channels, retained across turns, and later distort downstream behavior. To address this challenge, we propose MemPoison, a comprehensive benchmark and analysis framework featuring 1227 hand-validated cases across four attack types, three injection channels, and three representative memory substrates, evaluated on seven open-weight and three closed-weight model families. We introduce a three-tier taxonomy: (L1) direct single-record corruption, (L2) compositional multi-record corruption and (L3) context-triggered dormant corruption. Our evaluations reveal a distinct defense frontier: while baseline write-time defenses, such as consistency checks, substantially suppress direct L1 attacks, they fail to reliably suppress L2 and L3 attacks. Through mechanistic influence decomposition (MID), we demonstrate structural blind spots in write-time defenses, which admit seemingly benign records that later become harmful through joint retrieval composition or trigger-conditioned activation. Our findings advocate for shifting from static filtering to adaptive, context-sensitive memory defense strategies.

</details>


### [453] [Beyond Success Rate: Cost-Aware Evaluation of Offensive and Defensive Security Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.15263)
*Paul Kassianik,Blaine Nelson,Yaron Singer*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper evaluates security-agent performance by measuring success and cost across offensive and defensive tasks. Metrics focus on economic efficiency rather than peak capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To shift security-agent evaluation from emphasizing peak offensive capability to a realistic cost-success lens, assessing operational efficiency and fit for security tasks.

Method: Benchmarked language-model security agents on offensive Cybench CTF challenges and defensive Splunk BOTS v1 investigation tasks, analyzing performance based on inference expenditure, tool utilization, and operational feasibility.

Result: Offensive tasks showed performance improvement with additional computing resources, with open models nearing proprietary systems' capabilities. Defensive investigations relied more on tool usage and selective telemetry rather than raw computational power.

Conclusion: Benchmarks should integrate cost-aware and operationally relevant measures to determine practical applicability of security agents in real-world scenarios, particularly for defensive operations.

Abstract: Security-agent evaluations commonly measure peak offensive capability under generous inference budgets, emphasizing vulnerability discovery, exploit development, penetration testing, and CTF completion. Such measurements are useful but incomplete: in operational security, every reasoning step, tool call, telemetry query, and enrichment request consumes budget. We evaluate language-model security agents through this cost-success lens on offensive Cybench challenges and defensive Splunk BOTS v1 investigation challenges. Instead of reporting only best-case success, we compare models at fixed cost levels and decompose performance by inference spend and tool spend. Our results show distinct scalingregimes for red- and blue-team tasks. Offensive CTF performance improves with additional test-time compute, and scaled open-weight models can approach frontier proprietary systems while remaining cost-competitive. Defensive SOC investigation does not scale in the same way: success depends more heavily on disciplined tool use, telemetry navigation, and selective enrichment than on raw reasoning budget alone. We argue that security-agent benchmarks should measure economic efficiency and operational fit alongside task success. Cost-aware, SOC-native evaluations provide a clearer picture of which models are practically useful today and where defensive agents still need to improve. We present an interactive website with our results https://evals.frontier.security.

</details>


<div id='physics.ed-ph'></div>

# physics.ed-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [454] [Assessing AI in Introductory Physics Problem Solving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14303)
*Amir Bralin,N. Sanjay Rebello*

Main category: physics.ed-ph

TL;DR: The study evaluates OpenAI's o4-mini model on undergraduate-level physics problems, finding 90% overall accuracy but with performance variability based on problem modality and difficulty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess the problem-solving capabilities of the o4-mini model in physics by testing it on a widely used undergraduate textbook's problems.

Method: The study tested the o4-mini model using problems from Halliday and Resnick's "Fundamentals of Physics." Performance was analyzed based on modality (text-only vs. text-and-image) and difficulty level.

Result: The model achieved 90% overall accuracy, with higher success on text-only problems (96%) compared to text-with-image problems (79%), and accuracy decreased with increasing problem difficulty.

Conclusion: The o4-mini model demonstrates a strong capacity to solve introductory physics problems but faces challenges with multi-modality and complex problems.

Abstract: Reasoning or inference-scaling models are the new generation of Large Language Models (LLMs) capable of complex problem solving. To investigate their problem-solving capability in physics, we evaluated model o4-mini by OpenAI on solving traditional, end-of-chapter problems from Halliday and Resnick's "Fundamentals of Physics," spanning core topics in the undergraduate physics curriculum. Performance was analyzed across modality and problem difficulty. The model solved the problems with overall accuracy of about 90%, but performance depended strongly on representation: accuracy was much higher on text-only problems (96%) than on problems requiring coordinated interpretation of text and images (79%). Accuracy also declined significantly as the problem difficulty increased from low to medium to high. These results show that state-of-the-art LLMs can solve much of the standard introductory physics problems, but that their performance remains uneven and constrained by problem modality and problem difficulty.

</details>


<div id='eess.SY'></div>

# eess.SY [[Back]](#toc)

### [455] [Information-Theoretic Adaptive Cooling for Deterministic MPPI via Entropy Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.14245)
*Shuqi Wang,Wenrong Sun,Tao Han,Yue Gao,Xiang Yin*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper proposes an adaptive cooling framework, ITAC, for deterministic MPPI control using entropy-based feedback to improve efficiency and convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
